_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan (English I) style reading, batch F2. ~300-360 word abstract argumentative passages, 4 MCQs each (attitude/inference/main idea/argument/vocabulary). DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: Science commands, and deserves, an authority unrivalled among modern modes of knowing. Its method — the disciplined testing of conjecture against observation — has yielded an understanding of nature so powerful that to question its standing can seem mere obscurantism. Yet from this well-earned prestige a less defensible doctrine has grown, one that is easily confused with science but is in truth a philosophy about it. This doctrine, sometimes called scientism, holds that the methods of natural science are the only path to genuine knowledge, and that questions they cannot answer are not real questions at all. The claim is more sweeping than it first appears, and on inspection it proves self-undermining. For the assertion that only scientific knowledge is genuine is not itself a scientific finding; no experiment could establish it. It is a philosophical proposition, and one that, by its own standard, would have to be dismissed as meaningless. The doctrine thus saws off the branch on which it sits. More importantly, scientism mistakes the proper scope of a magnificent instrument. Science excels at telling us how the world is — what exists, how it behaves, by what mechanisms. It is silent, of necessity, on a whole range of questions that nonetheless press upon us: what we ought to do, what is worth valuing, what gives a life meaning. These are not failures of science to be remedied by more research; they lie outside its competence by their very nature. To acknowledge this is not to demote science but to understand it. The instrument is not diminished by recognising what it was not built to measure; it is the pretension to measure everything, and to dismiss whatever escapes the gauge, that betrays a misunderstanding of the very enterprise it claims to honour. To keep science within its proper bounds, then, is not to diminish it but to honour it the more; the very rigour that makes it sovereign within its own domain is precisely what disqualifies it from pronouncing on the questions of value that lie forever beyond that domain.

questions.prompt: What is 'scientism', as the writer defines it?
options: The disciplined testing of conjecture against observation.
The doctrine that scientific method is the only path to genuine knowledge.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call the doctrine self-undermining?
options: Because the claim that only science yields knowledge is not itself a scientific finding.

questions.prompt: On what kind of questions is science 'silent', according to the writer?
options: Questions of what we ought to do and what gives life meaning.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude towards science itself?
options: Respectful, while insisting on the proper limits of its scope.
Convinced it can answer every question.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 科学主义=唯科学方法才是真知之路)、细节(B '唯科学才是知识'本身不是科学结论、故自毁)、细节(B 科学对'应当做什么、何以有意义'保持沉默)、态度(B 尊重科学、但坚持其适用范围有界)。

sets.passage: To translate, it is often said, is to betray; and though the phrase is a commonplace, the truth it points to is profound and easily underestimated. The naive view supposes that translation is a mechanical substitution, a matter of replacing the words of one language with their equivalents in another, as one might exchange coins of different currencies at a fixed rate. On this view, a perfect translation is in principle always possible, and any failure is merely a want of skill. But languages are not interchangeable systems of labels attached to a common stock of meanings. Each carves the world differently, freighting its words with associations, rhythms and histories that have no precise counterpart elsewhere. A term that seems to translate cleanly may trail, in its original tongue, a cloud of connotation that vanishes in the passage, while the chosen equivalent imports associations of its own that the original never bore. Something is always lost, and something foreign always added. This does not make translation impossible, only imperfect in a way that no skill can wholly remedy. The translator is condemned to choose, at every turn, which of a passage's qualities to preserve at the expense of others — its literal sense or its music, its plain meaning or its tone, fidelity to the letter or fidelity to the spirit. The finest translations are not those that achieve an impossible perfection but those that choose their losses wisely, sacrificing the dispensable to save the essential. Far from a mechanical craft, translation is thus a sustained exercise of judgement, an art of intelligent compromise — and its necessary imperfection is not a scandal to be lamented but a consequence of the very richness and particularity of human languages. Every act of translation is thus a small lesson in the limits of language itself, a reminder that meaning is rooted in a particular soil and can never be transplanted whole, but only, with patience and loss, replanted in ground that subtly alters what grows there.

questions.prompt: What does the 'naive view' of translation suppose?
options: That it is a mechanical substitution of equivalent words.
That it is an art of compromise.
That languages carve the world differently.

questions.prompt: Why are languages not interchangeable, according to the writer?
options: Each carves the world differently, with associations that have no precise counterpart.

questions.prompt: What is the translator 'condemned' to do at every turn?
options: Choose which qualities to preserve at the expense of others.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's view of translation's imperfection?
options: A natural consequence of the richness of human languages, not a scandal.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 天真观:翻译是等值词的机械替换)、细节(B 各语言以不同方式切分世界、联想无精确对应)、细节(B 译者每步都被迫取舍保留哪些品质)、主旨/态度(B 不完美源于语言之丰富、非可悲丑闻)。

sets.passage: It is one of the quieter ironies of affluence that the multiplication of choices, long regarded as an unmixed good, may diminish rather than enhance our well-being. The assumption underlying modern consumer societies is straightforward: more options mean more freedom, and more freedom means more satisfaction, since each person may select precisely what suits him. Up to a point this is plainly true; a world with no choices would be a prison. But the relation does not hold without limit, and beyond a certain threshold the proliferation of options begins to exact costs that the simple equation overlooks. The first is the burden of deliberation itself. Every choice demands attention, comparison and decision, and when choices multiply beyond a manageable number, the effort of choosing comes to outweigh the value of choosing well. The second, subtler cost is the shadow cast by the roads not taken. With few options, the chooser who selects one forgoes little; with many, every selection means the renunciation of numerous alternatives, each of which might, for all one knows, have been better. The result is a persistent, low-grade regret, a sense that one's choice, however good, was probably not the best available. And the more freedom one had, the more keenly this disappointment is felt, for the responsibility for any shortfall falls squarely on the chooser. Freedom of choice, then, is a good that can be overdone, and the modern multiplication of options, pursued as though more were always better, may leave us not liberated but exhausted and faintly dissatisfied — richer in possibilities, but poorer in the contentment that a narrower world, paradoxically, more readily afforded. The remedy is not to abolish choice, which would be its own kind of tyranny, but to recognise that beyond a certain point the freedom to choose ceases to liberate and begins quietly to oppress, so that a life well lived may sometimes require us, deliberately, to choose to face fewer choices rather than more.

questions.prompt: What assumption about choice does the writer examine?
options: That more options mean more freedom and thus more satisfaction.

questions.prompt: What is the first cost of proliferating options?
options: The burden of deliberation, as the effort of choosing outweighs choosing well.

questions.prompt: What is the 'subtler cost' the writer describes?
options: A persistent regret cast by the alternatives one had to renounce.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's central conclusion?
options: Freedom of choice cannot be overdone.
Freedom of choice is a good that can be overdone, leaving us exhausted and dissatisfied.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 假设:选项越多=自由越多=满足越多)、细节(B 第一代价:权衡之累、择优之力超过其值)、细节(B 更微妙代价:被放弃的备选投下持久遗憾)、主旨(B 选择自由可被过度、令人疲惫而微不满)。

sets.passage: The great city offers its inhabitants a gift so familiar that they seldom recognise it as one: the gift of anonymity. To walk through a crowded metropolis is to move among thousands who neither know nor care who one is, and in this indifference, oppressive though it can seem, lies a peculiar kind of liberation. The villager lives under the constant, mild surveillance of neighbours who have known him from birth, whose expectations enclose him as surely as any wall; the city-dweller, by contrast, may reinvent himself, shed inherited roles, and pursue a life of his own choosing unobserved by anyone with a stake in keeping him what he was. Yet this same anonymity is the source of the city's characteristic discontents. The freedom from the watchful community is also a loss of its support; the stranger who may do as he pleases is also the stranger to whom no one owes anything, who may fall ill or into despair unnoticed amid the multitude. The indifference that liberates is the same indifference that isolates. This double aspect is not a defect to be engineered away but the very texture of urban life, which trades the warmth and the constraint of the small community for the freedom and the loneliness of the crowd. Those who celebrate the city for its opportunities and those who lament it for its coldness are describing, from opposite sides, a single condition. To understand the city is to grasp that its liberating anonymity and its isolating indifference are not two things but one — that the very feature which makes the metropolis a place of self-invention makes it also a place where one may be, in the midst of millions, profoundly alone. To live well in a great city, then, is to hold its two faces together: to draw on the freedom its indifference grants without surrendering to the isolation that is freedom's shadow, and to build, within the anonymous crowd, the small circles of recognition that the city permits but does not provide.

questions.prompt: What 'gift' does the great city offer, according to the writer?
options: The warmth of a small community.

questions.prompt: How does anonymity liberate the city-dweller?
options: By allowing self-reinvention unobserved by those with a stake in his past.
By introducing him to all neighbours.

questions.prompt: What is the source of the city's 'characteristic discontents'?
options: The same anonymity that liberates also isolates and withdraws support.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's central insight about the city?
options: Its freedom and its loneliness are two separate things.
Its liberating anonymity and isolating indifference are one and the same condition.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 城市之赠礼是匿名)、细节(B 匿名让人不受旧关系注视而自我重塑)、细节(B 解放人的冷漠同时也孤立人、撤走支持)、主旨(B 解放性匿名与孤立性冷漠本是一体)。

sets.passage: There is a growing suspicion, hard to dismiss, that the technologies of instant access are quietly reshaping the human capacity for patience, and with it for the sustained attention on which so much that is valuable depends. The complaint is not new in form — every age has fretted that its novelties were corrupting the young — but the particular charge deserves more than reflexive scepticism. When information of every kind is available at once, and when each pause is an invitation to a fresh stimulus a thumb's breadth away, the slow and effortful forms of attention may simply fall into disuse. We grow accustomed to the immediate, and the immediate, by degrees, becomes the only thing we can comfortably tolerate. What is at stake is more than a fashion in habits. The deepest rewards of human life — the mastery of a difficult skill, the understanding of a demanding book, the cultivation of a friendship — are not available to the impatient. They yield only to a sustained and unrewarded effort, to a willingness to dwell with difficulty before the dividend arrives. A sensibility trained to expect instant gratification is, almost by definition, disqualified from these goods, not because it lacks the capacity but because it has lost the habit of patience through which alone the capacity is exercised. The danger, then, is not that we will know less — we may, in a shallow sense, know more than ever — but that we will become incapable of the depth that patience makes possible. To preserve that depth in an age engineered for distraction is not a matter of nostalgia but of cultivating, deliberately and against the grain, a discipline that our tools no longer impose and increasingly conspire to dissolve. The cultivation of patience, on this view, is not a quaint virtue left over from a slower age but an act of quiet resistance, and perhaps the precondition of every depth — of thought, of feeling, of friendship — that a life engineered for perpetual distraction would otherwise place beyond our reach.

questions.prompt: What 'suspicion' does the writer take seriously?
options: That it is reshaping our capacity for patience and sustained attention.
That every age improves the young.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say the rewards of life are unavailable to the impatient?
options: Because they yield only to sustained, unrewarded effort before the dividend arrives.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say is the real danger?
options: That we will know far less than before.
That we will become incapable of the depth that patience makes possible.

questions.prompt: What does preserving 'depth' require, according to the writer?
options: Deliberately cultivating patience against the grain of our tools.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 即时获取技术正重塑耐心与专注)、细节(B 深层回报只对'先与困难厮守'的持久努力开放)、细节(B 真正危险是丧失耐心所成就的深度)、主旨(B 须逆潮流刻意修炼耐心之纪律)。

sets.passage: The ideal of authenticity — of being true to one's real self rather than conforming to external expectation — has become so central to the modern moral imagination that it is rarely questioned. To call a person authentic is among the highest praise we offer; to call them false or conformist, among our sharper rebukes. Yet the ideal, examined, rests on assumptions that do not bear much weight. It presupposes that each of us possesses a real self, fixed and waiting to be discovered, which our social roles and obligations merely overlay or suppress. On this picture, the task of life is a kind of excavation: to strip away the false accretions of convention and lay bare the genuine person beneath. But the self, more plausibly, is not a buried object but a construction, shaped through and through by the very relationships, languages and traditions that the doctrine of authenticity dismisses as external impositions. There is no untutored self prior to all influence, waiting to be uncovered; there is only the self that culture and circumstance have made, and that we, in turn, continue to make through our choices. If this is so, the injunction to be true to oneself loses its apparent clarity. To which self should one be true — the self one happens to be, or the self one might become? And by what standard, if not the very conventions authenticity scorns, is the 'real' self to be distinguished from the false? The ideal, far from offering firm guidance, conceals a vacancy at its heart. This is not to dismiss it entirely, for the impulse it expresses — against a hollow and unreflective conformity — is sound. But it cannot bear the weight the modern world has placed upon it, and a maturity worth the name must look beyond it. Such a maturity will not ask the impossible question of which buried self to obey, but will accept the harder and more hopeful task of making a self worth being — not by excavating a hidden essence but by deliberate construction, out of the very materials the cult of authenticity teaches us to despise.

questions.prompt: What assumption does the ideal of authenticity rest on?
options: That the self is a construction.
That each of us has a fixed real self waiting to be discovered.
That culture shapes the self entirely.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's alternative view of the self?
options: A construction shaped by relationships, languages and traditions.
A fixed essence prior to influence.

questions.prompt: Why does 'be true to oneself' lose its clarity?
options: Because it is unclear which self, and by what standard, the 'real' self is identified.
Because no one has a self.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's final position on authenticity?
options: Its impulse against hollow conformity is sound, but it cannot bear the weight placed on it.
It is a perfect and complete guide.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 本真性预设各人有待发现的固定真我)、细节(B 作者认为自我是被关系语言传统塑造的建构)、细节(B '忠于自我'不清楚指哪个自我、以何标准)、主旨/态度(B 其反对空洞从众的冲动可取、但承载不了被赋予的重量)。

sets.passage: Modern societies are often described as uniquely preoccupied with risk, and the description, though sometimes offered as a reproach, points to something real and worth understanding. Earlier ages were not, of course, free of danger; they faced famine, plague and violence on a scale we can scarcely imagine. But they tended to regard such calamities as fate, or as the will of providence — as belonging to an order of things that lay beyond human control and therefore, in a sense, beyond human responsibility. The modern attitude is profoundly different. As our power to predict and to intervene has grown, misfortunes that were once accepted as inevitable have been reclassified as risks: as outcomes that might, in principle, be foreseen, managed and averted by sufficient care. This transformation is in many ways a triumph, and it has saved countless lives. But it carries a hidden psychological cost that is seldom reckoned. For when a misfortune is understood as a risk that ought to have been managed, its occurrence invites not resignation but blame; someone, it is felt, must have failed to take the precautions that would have prevented it. The very competence that allows us to control so much breeds an expectation of control that reality cannot satisfy, and every residual disaster becomes an occasion for recrimination and a search for the negligent. We are, in consequence, both safer and more anxious than our ancestors — protected from dangers they endured, yet burdened by a sense of responsibility, and a vulnerability to blame, from which their fatalism, for all its resignation, mercifully shielded them. The price of our remarkable mastery, in short, is a peculiarly modern unease: having banished fate from our account of the world, we have made ourselves answerable for very nearly everything, and a society in which every misfortune must be somebody's fault is, for all its genuine safety, a strangely anxious and unforgiving place in which to live.

questions.prompt: How did earlier ages tend to regard calamities?
options: As fate or providence, beyond human control and responsibility.
As the fault of negligent individuals.

questions.prompt: What is the 'modern attitude' to misfortune?
options: Reclassifying misfortunes as risks that might be foreseen and averted.

questions.prompt: What 'hidden psychological cost' does the writer identify?
options: Misfortune invites blame, as someone is felt to have failed to take precautions.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's central claim about modern people?
options: They are both safer and more anxious than their ancestors.
They are exactly like their ancestors.
They are less safe and less anxious.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 旧时把灾难视为命运/天意、超出人之控制与责任)、细节(B 现代把不幸重归类为可预见可规避的风险)、细节(B 隐性代价:不幸招致归咎、必有人疏于防范)、主旨(A 现代人比祖先更安全也更焦虑)。

sets.passage: There is a kind of knowledge that justifies itself by its uses — that we pursue because it enables us to cure a disease, build a bridge or increase a harvest — and in an age impatient of anything that does not pay, this is the only kind whose value is readily conceded. But there is another kind, pursued for no end beyond the understanding it brings, and its defenders have always had to argue, against a sceptical practicality, that it is worth anything at all. The argument is more than antiquarian piety. In the first place, the sharp line between useful and useless knowledge dissolves on inspection. The most consequential applications have again and again grown from inquiries undertaken with no practical aim whatever — abstract speculations in mathematics or physics, pursued for their own sake, that lay apparently idle for generations before transforming the material world beyond their authors' imagining. A civilisation that funded only research of foreseeable utility would, paradoxically, forfeit the unforeseeable discoveries on which its future utility most depends. But the deeper defence does not rest on this happy accident. It holds that understanding is a good in itself, that to comprehend the world we inhabit — its origins, its structure, its strange order — is part of what it is to live a fully human life, as much as health or comfort. To value knowledge only for its uses is to treat the mind as a mere instrument of the body's needs, and to miss the fact that the desire to know, disinterested and unappeasable, is among the noblest and most distinctively human of impulses. A society that cannot see this has grown not merely poorer but smaller, having reduced one of the great ends of life to a mere means. To defend disinterested inquiry is not to oppose the useful but to remember that the human mind was not made solely to serve the body's needs, and that a civilisation which forgets this has not merely surrendered a luxury but mislaid one of the very ends for which prosperity itself was always meant to be the means.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say about the line between useful and useless knowledge?
What paradox does the writer note about funding only useful research?
options: It would forfeit the unforeseeable discoveries on which future utility most depends.
It produces no knowledge at all.

questions.prompt: What is the 'deeper defence' of useless knowledge?
options: That understanding is a good in itself, part of a fully human life.

questions.prompt: What does valuing knowledge only for its uses miss, according to the writer?
options: That the disinterested desire to know is among the noblest human impulses.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 有用/无用知识的界限一审即溶)、细节(B 只资助有用研究反会失去未来效用所依赖的意外发现)、细节(B 更深辩护:理解本身即善、是完整人生之一部)、主旨(B 只以用途衡量知识、错失求知这一最高贵的人类冲动)。

sets.passage: Every living tradition faces a tension it can never finally resolve, between the impulse to preserve what it has inherited and the necessity of adapting to changed conditions. The conservative instinct sees in inherited forms the distilled wisdom of generations, a inheritance too precious and too little understood to be tampered with lightly; the innovating instinct sees in them the dead hand of the past, obstructing the improvements that present circumstances demand. Each, pressed to an extreme, is ruinous. A tradition that refuses all change petrifies; clinging to forms whose original purpose has lapsed, it becomes a museum of empty observances, and is eventually discarded as irrelevant by those it can no longer serve. But a tradition that embraces every novelty dissolves; abandoning whatever the moment finds inconvenient, it forfeits the continuity that made it a tradition at all, and leaves its heirs adrift, severed from the accumulated understanding that is a tradition's chief gift. The health of a tradition lies, then, in a difficult and unending negotiation between these poles — in the capacity to change while remaining recognisably itself, to adapt its forms without betraying its substance, to distinguish the essential, which must be preserved at any cost, from the incidental, which may be altered without loss. This is not a problem to be solved once and filed away but a judgement to be exercised afresh in every generation, and exercised, moreover, without any formula to guarantee the right answer. The traditions that endure are not those that cling rigidly nor those that yield indiscriminately, but those that have mastered this most delicate of arts: the art of changing in order to remain the same. The wisest custodians of any tradition are therefore neither the reactionaries who would freeze it in place nor the iconoclasts who would discard it wholesale, but those who grasp that fidelity to a living thing sometimes requires changing it, so that it may be, in each new age, thoughtfully reinterpreted rather than merely repeated by rote.

questions.prompt: What tension does every living tradition face?
options: Between preserving its inheritance and adapting to changed conditions.

questions.prompt: What happens to a tradition that refuses all change?
options: It petrifies, becoming a museum of empty observances eventually discarded.

questions.prompt: What happens to a tradition that embraces every novelty?
options: It dissolves, forfeiting the continuity that made it a tradition.

questions.prompt: Where does the writer say the health of a tradition lies?
options: In clinging rigidly to all forms.
In changing while remaining recognisably itself, preserving substance over incidentals.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 张力在保存遗产与适应变局之间)、细节(B 拒变之传统僵化成空仪式之博物馆而被弃)、细节(B 全纳新奇之传统消解、失去延续性)、主旨(B 健康在于'变中守恒'、保本质而改枝节)。

sets.passage: We are inclined to judge people, and to judge ourselves, as though the moral worth of an action depended wholly on the will behind it, and not at all on the outcomes that fortune appends. The intuition is a deep one and seems, at first, unimpeachable: a person can be praised or blamed only for what lies within his control, and since the results of his actions depend on countless circumstances he cannot govern, it would be unjust to let those results affect our verdict on him. And yet our actual practice of judgement flatly contradicts this principle, in ways we find almost impossible to abandon. Two drivers may be equally careless; the one whose lapse happens to kill a child is condemned as a criminal, while the other, whose identical lapse harms no one only because the road was empty, is barely reproached. The difference between them lies entirely in factors beyond their control — in luck — and yet we cannot bring ourselves to judge them alike. This phenomenon, which philosophers have called moral luck, exposes a genuine contradiction at the heart of our moral thinking, one that resists every attempt at tidy resolution. We are committed both to the principle that people should be judged only for what they control, and to a practice of judgement that constantly violates it; and neither the principle nor the practice can be surrendered without doing violence to convictions we hold equally dear. The honest conclusion may be that our moral life is simply not the coherent system we should like it to be — that it rests on commitments which, though each is compelling in isolation, cannot all be true together. To recognise this is uncomfortable, but it is more honest than the false tidiness that pretends the tension away. Perhaps the most that honesty allows is to hold the contradiction steadily before us, letting it temper both our condemnation of others and our pride in ourselves, and resisting the perennial temptation to buy a false peace of mind by pretending that a tension built into the very foundations of morality is not really there.

questions.prompt: What intuition about moral judgement does the writer begin with?
options: That a person can be judged only for what lies within his control.
That no one can be judged.

questions.prompt: What does the example of the two careless drivers illustrate?
options: That carelessness is always punished equally.
That we judge identically careless people differently because of luck.

questions.prompt: What does 'moral luck' expose, according to the writer?
options: A genuine contradiction at the heart of our moral thinking.

questions.prompt: What 'honest conclusion' does the writer reach?
options: Our moral life is a perfectly coherent system.
Our moral life rests on commitments that cannot all be true together.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 直觉:人只能就其可控之事被评判)、细节(B 两个同样粗心的司机因运气被不同评判)、细节(B 道德运气暴露道德思维核心的真矛盾)、主旨/态度(B 诚实结论:道德生活的诸承诺无法同时为真)。
